coredata.dc:title: Enhanced Z-scheme ZnS/NH2−MIL-125(Ti) photocatalysts with biomass-derived carbon quantum dots for CO2 reduction
coredata.dc:creator: Zhou, Jun
Jia, Qinwei
Gong, Lei
Zhang, Shijun
Zhou, Xiangyu
Song, Hanwen
coredata.dc:description: The conversion of CO2 and H2O into hydrocarbon fuels using solar energy represents a promising approach to artificial photosynthesis. However, the rapid recombination of photogenerated carriers presents a significant obstacle to the development of this technology. In this study, we extracted carbon quantum dots (CQDs) from waste biomass sludge and utilized them as electron transfer mediators between ZnS and NH2−MIL-125(Ti) for CO2 photoreduction. Comparative analysis with single-phase and binary composites revealed that the ZnS/CQDs/NH2−MIL-125(Ti) composites exhibited heightened photocatalytic activity and improved photostability. Electrochemical and optical investigations further elucidated that the incorporation of CQDs enhanced the charge separation efficiency and visible light responsiveness of the composites. The yields of CH3OH and CH3CH2OH achieved were 501.57 µmol/g and 564.70 µmol/g, respectively, following 3 h of visible light irradiation. This study presents a novel reference for utilizing biomass to facilitate the conversion of CO2 and H2O into valuable chemicals.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Photocatalysis
CO2 reduction
Waste sludge treatment
Z-scheme

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the preparation process for ZCNM composites.
SEM image of prepared samples: (a) 2ZCNM; TEM images of prepared samples (b) NM, (c) CQDs, (d) and (e) 2ZCNM; (f) HRTEM image of the 2ZCNM; (g) EDS elemental maps of C, Ti, S and Zn.
(a) XRD patterns of the NM, CNM and 2ZCNM; (b) FT-IR spectra of NM, CNM and 2ZCNM; (c) N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms of NM and 2ZCNM.
XPS spectra of the 2ZCNM: (a) survey, (b) C 1 s, (c) O 1 s, (d) N 1 s, (e) Ti 2p, (f) Zn 2p, and (g) S 2p.

(a) CH3OH and CH3CH2OH yield for various photocatalysts; (b) production rate of CH3OH and CH3CH2OH; (c) time-course evolution of the product over the 2ZCNM photocatalyst; (d) recycling experiments of photocatalytic reduction of CO2 over 2ZCNM.
(a) Photocurrent response; (b) EIS Nyquist; (c) PL spectra; (d) UV–Vis DRS spectra.
(a) The band gap of the ZnS and NM; (b) The valance band VB-XPS of the ZnS; (c) VB-XPS spectra of NM; (d) ESR spectra by adding DMPO to capture •O2 − with NM, ZnS, and 2ZCNM.
Comparison of photocatalytic CO2 reduction of ZnS/CQDs/NH2−MIL-125(Ti) with other photocatalysts.

head.ce:title: Enhanced Z-scheme ZnS/NH2−MIL-125(Ti) photocatalysts with biomass-derived carbon quantum dots for CO2 reduction

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Environmental and Safety Engineering, Qingdao University of Science and Technology, 53 Zhengzhou Road, Qingdao, Shandong Province 266042, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Environmental and Safety Engineering
Qingdao University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aCollege of Environmental and Safety Engineering, Qingdao University of Science and Technology, 53 Zhengzhou Road, Qingdao, Shandong Province 266042, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: HSE Department of Engineering Construction Management Center, Wanhua Chemical Group Co.,Ltd., 3 Sanya Road, Yantai, Shandong Province 266699, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: HSE Department of Engineering Construction Management Center

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bHSE Department of Engineering Construction Management Center, Wanhua Chemical Group Co.,Ltd., 3 Sanya Road, Yantai, Shandong Province 266699, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The conversion of CO2 and H2O into hydrocarbon fuels using solar energy represents a promising approach to artificial photosynthesis. However, the rapid recombination of photogenerated carriers presents a significant obstacle to the development of this technology. In this study, we extracted carbon quantum dots (CQDs) from waste biomass sludge and utilized them as electron transfer mediators between ZnS and NH2−MIL-125(Ti) for CO2 photoreduction. Comparative analysis with single-phase and binary composites revealed that the ZnS/CQDs/NH2−MIL-125(Ti) composites exhibited heightened photocatalytic activity and improved photostability. Electrochemical and optical investigations further elucidated that the incorporation of CQDs enhanced the charge separation efficiency and visible light responsiveness of the composites. The yields of CH3OH and CH3CH2OH achieved were 501.57 µmol/g and 564.70 µmol/g, respectively, following 3 h of visible light irradiation. This study presents a novel reference for utilizing biomass to facilitate the conversion of CO2 and H2O into valuable chemicals.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The overconsumption of fossil fuels and the resulting high levels of CO2 emissions have led to global energy and environmental challenges. As a result, extensive research has been conducted to explore renewable alternatives to fossil fuels [1–5]. Photocatalytic reduction of CO2 to hydrocarbon fuels has emerged as a particularly promising option [6,7]. Numerous semiconductors, such as TiO2 [8–12], CdS [13–16] and g-C3N4 [17–20], have been investigated for promoting the photocatalytic CO2 conversion. However, the existing catalysts for CO2 photocatalysis face challenges, including high cost, low efficiency, and poor stability [21]. Therefore, the development of efficient photocatalysts for CO2 reduction remains a challenging and long-term endeavor [22,23].

Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) are porous crystalline materials with semiconductor-like properties that have gained significant attention in various photocatalytic reactions due to their tunable porosity, large specific surface area, and unique structures [24–26]. NH2−MIL-125(Ti) is an early-reported MOF for CO2 photoreduction, sharing a similar structure with MIL-125(Ti) and consisting of titanium oxide clusters and 2-Aminoterephthalic Acid [27–29]. Previous studies have demonstrated that introducing LiFePO4 into Ti-based MOFs can significantly enhance the photocatalytic CO2 reduction activity under visible light, thanks to the insertion of Li+ and chemical morphological changes of Fe2+ [30]. Despite its potential, NH2−MIL-125(Ti) still encounters challenges related to charge carrier recombination. ZnS has emerged as a highly efficient wide bandgap semiconductor photocatalyst due to its cost-effectiveness, unique energy band structure, and high photocatalytic activity [31]. However, electron backflow in ZnS can lead to photocorrosion and electron-hole pair recombination, hampering its overall performance [32–34]. To address these issues, coupling ZnS with NH2−MIL-125(Ti) in a heterostructure photocatalyst can effectively compensate for the shortcomings and improve overall performance [35]. Additionally, the incorporation of electron transfer mediators has been identified as an effective approach to enhance catalytic activity and promote efficient electron-hole separation [36–38]. In our previous studies, we used Pt NPs as "conductors" to construct efficient heterostructures, leading to improved photocatalytic efficiency of NH2−MIL-125(Ti) for the production of CH3OH and CH3CH2OH [39]. However, precious metals are typically expensive. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) offer a low-cost alternative due to their small size, low toxicity, excellent biocompatibility, chemical inertness, and optical performance advantages [40–42]. Currently, CQDs are commonly derived from carbon-containing sources [43]. Carbohydrate-rich waste biomass sludge from secondary sedimentation tanks presents a potential raw material for CQDs preparation [2]. Purification processes yield CQDs with additional fluorescent functional groups for improved conductivity and photoluminescence. This approach offers the advantage of simultaneously treating waste sludge and promoting charge transfer at the heterostructure, providing a dual benefit [44,45].

In this study, we synthesized Z-scheme heterostructure photocatalysts, ZnS/CQDs/NH2−MIL-125(Ti), utilizing CQDs derived from waste biomass sludge. These composite catalysts exhibited a significant enhancement in the photocatalytic conversion efficiency of CO2 to CH3OH and CH3CH2OH under visible light. Extensive characterization of the composites was conducted, including the investigation of their morphology, structure, composition, electrochemical, and optical properties. The role of each component in the photocatalytic CO2 reduction process was thoroughly discussed, shedding light on their contributions. Furthermore, a possible mechanism for the photocatalytic reduction of CO2 was derived from the analysis of the energy band structure.

NH2−MIL-125(Ti) was synthesized following a previously reported method [46]. Briefly, 1.57 mL of butyl titanate and 3.24 g of 2-Aminoterephthalic Acid were dissolved in a mixture of 6 mL of methanol and 54 mL of N, N-Dimethylformamide (DMF) with vigorous stirring. After stirring for 1 h, the resulting suspension was transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and maintained at 150 °C for 24 h. Subsequently, the mixture was cooled to room temperature, washed alternately with methanol and DMF, and subjected to vacuum drying at 60 °C for 12 h. For simplicity, the resulting product will be referred to as NM (short for NH2−MIL-125(Ti)).

The synthesis of CQDs was adapted and improved from previous studies [2]. Initially, 3.0 g of dry waste sludge from a secondary settling tank was dissolved in 60 mL of deionized water and sonicated until homogeneous at room temperature. The resulting mixture was transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 12 h. After cooling, centrifugation was performed three times at 10,000 rpm to obtain the CQDs solution, which was then filtered through a 0.45 µm filter to remove precipitated impurities, resulting in a yellowish-brown upper layer. Subsequently, 9 mL of the CQDs solution was mixed with 30 mL of deionized water, and 0.4 g of NM was added and stirred for 1 h in the dark. The mixture was centrifuged, rinsed with deionized water, and dried under vacuum at 60 °C for 12 h. The resulting product will be referred to as CNM (standing for CQDs/NH2−MIL-125(Ti)).

To synthesize the composite materials, a mixture of Zn(OAc)2 and thioacetamide in 60 mL of deionized water was prepared with a 1:1 molar ratio. Next, 0.4 g of CNM was added to the mixture and stirred until uniformly dispersed. The resulting mixture was transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 150 ºC for 8 h. Subsequently, the composite materials were washed with water/ethanol and dried under vacuum at 60 ºC for 12 h. The obtained composites contained different weight percentages of ZnS, including 1 wt%, 2 wt%, 3 wt%, and 4 wt%, denoted as ZCNM. As a comparison, ZnS monomer materials were synthesized following a similar procedure without the addition of CNM. Furthermore, a ZnS/NH2−MIL-125(Ti) composite, denoted as ZNM, was prepared by replacing CNM with NM during the synthesis process. The overall photocatalyst preparation process used in this study is depicted in Fig.1 .

Photocatalytic performance was investigated using a 250 mL quartz reaction vessel (Beijing Zhongjiao Jinyuan Co., Ltd, 250 mL). Initially, 100 mL of deionized water was added to the vessel. Subsequently, 100 mg of the photocatalyst was mixed well with 100 mL of deionized water. To remove any air effects, high-purity CO2 (99.99 %) was continuously passed through the suspension at a flow rate of 50 mL/min for at least 30 min before the reaction. Light simulation was achieved using a 300 W xenon arc lamp (PLS-SXE300C, Beijing Perfect Light Technology Co., Ltd., Beijing, China) equipped with a 420 nm cut-off filter. The temperature of the photoreaction was maintained at approximately 15 °C using a cooling water circuit. Samples were collected at regular intervals of 0.5 h over a 3 h period during the photocatalytic reaction. The reaction solution was centrifuged, and the liquid sample was analyzed using gas chromatography (Shimadzu GC-2014C, FID detector, FFAP column 30 m × 0.25 mm × 0.25 µm). Control experiments were also conducted, including N2 substitution for CO2, removal of light, or catalyst elimination. The most effective samples were further evaluated for cycling performance through a series of centrifugation, rinsing with deionized water, vacuum drying at 60 °C overnight, and repeating the process five times with deionized water.

The morphologies of NM, CQD, and 2ZCNM were characterized using scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As shown in Fig. 2a , 2ZCNM exhibited a decahedral structure with dimensions ranging from 400 to 800 nm, featuring irregular particle attachment and a rough surface. Fig. 2b shows the ortho-decahedral structure of NM with flat edges. The CQDs solution, obtained through the hydrothermal extraction method from biomass, is displayed in Fig. 2c, exhibiting a size range of 2–8 nm. In Fig. 2d, the edges of 2ZCNM displayed noticeable roughness and distinct particles. Fig. 2e highlights the presence of clearly visible CQDs and edge-loaded ZnS grains. The grains at the edges of 2ZCNM were further observed using high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM), as depicted in Fig. 2f. The results revealed lattice stripes with crystal plane spacing of 0.160 nm and 0.190 nm, corresponding to the (008) and (106) crystal planes of ZnS, respectively. In conclusion, CQDs were successfully prepared, establishing close contact between CQDs, ZnS, and NM. The EDS images of the 2ZCNM catalyst were shown in Fig.2 g. It could be seen that the C, Ti, S and Zn elements were uniformly distributed.

The crystal structures of NM, 2ZCNM, and ZnS were investigated through X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis. As shown in Fig. 3a , NM exhibited characteristic signal peaks at 6.75°, 9.70°, 11.62°, 13.53°, 16.52°, 17.87°, 18.95°, and 19.56°, consistent with previous literature [47,48]. The strong intensity of the diffraction peaks indicated a high degree of crystallinity and purity of the NM material, without any TiO2 impurity peaks [48]. In the spectra of 2ZCNM, signal peaks corresponding to the (111), (200), and (220) crystal planes of ZnS were observed at 28.55°, 33.09°, and 47.51°, respectively. However, due to the small size or low loading of the CQDs, the diffraction peaks of ZnS were partially obscured by NM. It is noteworthy that after the loading of CQDs and ZnS, the positions of the signal peaks did not show significant shifts. The diffraction peaks remained narrow and sharp, indicating the successful synthesis of the composite catalyst.

To gain deeper insights into the functional groups and chemical bonds, Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) analysis was conducted, and the results are presented in Fig. 3b. In all photocatalyst samples, the absorption peak observed at 3446 cm−1 was attributed to the symmetric stretching vibration of -NH2, which could potentially overlap with the absorption peak of OH vibrations caused by the presence of moisture in the samples [39]. The absorption peaks observed at 1470–1720 cm−1 and 1340–1450 cm−1 were assigned to the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of carboxylate functional groups, respectively [49]. Additionally, the absorption peak at 1250 cm−1 was associated with the in-plane bending vibration of the NC bond in the benzene ring [50]. Furthermore, the absorption peak at 772 cm−1 corresponded to the characteristic absorption of O-Ti-O stretching vibrations [51,52]. Notably, the absorption intensity at 620 cm−1 was significantly enhanced in the case of 2ZCNM, indicating the characteristic absorption of ZnS at this particular frequency.

Fig. 3c illustrates the adsorption-desorption isotherms of N2 on NM and 2ZCNM. According to the classification of adsorption isotherms by the IUPAC, the isotherm of NM belongs to Type-I, consistent with previous literature [53]. The BET surface area and pore volume of NM were determined to be 908.60 m2·g−1 and 0.509 cm3·g−1 respectively, indicating the successful formation of a microporous framework. On the other hand, 2ZCNM exhibited a typical Type-IV adsorption isotherm, indicating the presence of mesoporous structures in addition to the occurrence of Type-I adsorption in slit-like pores. The H3 hysteresis loop observed in the isotherm of 2ZCNM suggested the presence of mesopores. The BET surface area and pore volume of 2ZCNM were measured as 407.79 m2·g−1 and 0.280 cm3·g−1, respectively. The decrease in specific surface area relative to NM was attributed to the coverage of some pores by the loaded CQDs and ZnS, leading to changes in the pore structure and the presence of certain mesoporous structures. These mesoporous structures are beneficial for the adsorption of H2O and CO2 molecules during the photocatalytic process, enhancing mass transfer effects.

XPS analysis was conducted to elucidate the composition and chemical state of the composite catalyst, 2ZCNM. Fig. 4a showcased the six distinct sets of signal peaks corresponding to Zn 2p, O 1 s, Ti 2p, N 1 s, C 1 s, and S 2p electronic orbitals. The XPS spectrum of C 1 s (Fig. 4b) revealed three peaks at 284.8, 286.3, and 288.8 eV, corresponding to C = C, CO, and C = O bonds, respectively, characteristic of NM. The C = C bond arose from the benzoic acid ring, while the CO and C = O bonds primarily originated from H2ATA linkers in NM [54]. Moving to the O 1 s spectrum (Fig. 4c), peaks at 531.9 and 533.3 eV represented CO and C = O, respectively [28]. The N 1 s spectrum (Fig. 4d) exhibited a peak at 398.9 eV corresponding to -NH2 and another at 401.6 eV associated with -N=+ or -NH-+ [54]. The Ti 2p spectrum revealed two signal peaks at 459.1 and 464.8 eV, corresponding to Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2, respectively, indicating the presence of Ti in the +4 valence state within the Ti-O cluster, consistent with NM (Fig. 4e) [55]. Regarding Zn, the binding energies of Zn 2p3/2 and Zn 2p1/2 were measured at 1022.2 eV and 1045.2 eV, respectively, suggesting the presence of Zn2+ and confirming that it did not interact with the N ligand amino group during the loading process (Fig. 4f) [34]. The S 2p spectrum displayed two peaks at 161.7 and 162.9 eV, corresponding to S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2, respectively (Fig. 4 g). These results demonstrate the successful synthesis of 2ZCNM nanocomposites.

The yield curve of CO2 photocatalytic reduction by ZCNM is depicted in Fig 5c. As the light reaction progresses, the production of CH3OH and CH3CH2OH gradually increases, but the reaction rate decreases. This decrease in rate could be attributed to the pre-adsorption process prior to light irradiation, which results in a substantial amount of CO2 and H2O molecules being adsorbed on the catalyst surface. During the first hour of the reaction, a high reaction rate is sustained, followed by a slight decrease in rate as the reaction proceeds. To ascertain the reproducibility of the photocatalytic reduction, the experiment was repeated under identical conditions. As illustrated in Fig. 5d, after 5 cycles, there was no significant reduction in the production of CH3OH and CH3CH2OH, indicating minimal loss of photocatalytic activity and underscoring the excellent repeatability of 2ZCNM.

We further investigated the sources of its excellent performance through transient photocurrent response, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), and photoluminescence (PL) analyses. As shown in Fig. 6a , significant photocurrent intensities were observed during the on-off cycles of each switch in the same irradiation process. Due to the substantial aggregation and recombination of photo-induced charge carriers, both NM and ZnS exhibited low photocurrent intensities. The increased photocurrent density of 2ZCNM could be attributed to the accelerated electron transfer to ZnS facilitated by CQDs, leading to higher photocurrent output as ZnS accepts the electrons. Furthermore, the Nyquist plot of the samples in Fig. 6b indicated that 2ZCNM had the smallest charge transfer resistance, suggesting a faster interface charge transfer rate [63]. The emission spectra of the samples, as shown in Fig. 6c, revealed a strong emission peak at 430 nm for the individual catalysts, indicating the rapid coupling of electrons and holes. The reduced PL peak of CNM could be attributed to the presence of CQDs, which act as electron conductors and facilitate the migration and storage of photo-generated electrons. The PL results of 2ZNM further confirmed the acceptance of electrons by ZnS from NM, effectively suppressing carrier recombination [2]. The weakest PL emission intensity of 2ZCNM indicated the longest carrier lifetime, suggesting that the enhancement of photocatalytic activity in 2ZCNM is consistent with the improvement in the efficiency of photogenerated charge carrier separation [64]. The absorption edges of NM and ZnS were found to be approximately 507 nm and 388 nm, respectively, as demonstrated in Fig. 6d. ZnS exhibited low absorbance in the visible region. The visible absorption range of 2ZCNM was extended to 556 nm, which was the broadest range observed. This broad range allowed for the capture of more photons, resulting in the formation of more electrons.

To further investigate the electron transfer pathways in the composite catalyst 2ZCNM, the gap energy (Eg) of NM and ZnS were analyzed based on the UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS) test results. The Tauc-plot formula was used to obtain the equation (αhv)² = A(hv - Eg)ⁿ, where α, h, v, and A represent the absorption coefficient, Planck's constant, light frequency, and constant, respectively [65]. Since the electron transition forms for both NM and ZnS are direct transitions, the value of n is 1. As shown in Fig. 7a , the Eg of NM and ZnS were determined to be 2.68 eV and 3.40 eV, respectively. In this study, the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) energy of NM and the valence band (VB) energy of ZnS were analyzed based on the VB-XPS results to further determine the energy band structure of the composite catalyst. As shown in Fig. 7b, the HOMO energy of NM was determined to be 2.36 eV, and the VB energy of ZnS was 1.71 eV. The lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) energy and conduction band energy (CB) energy can be calculated using the formula
E g
=
E
V B
−
E
C B
[63]. The LUMO energy of NM was found to be −0.32 eV, while the CB energy of ZnS was determined to be −1.69 eV.

The results mentioned above demonstrate that the energy bands of NM and ZnS exhibit a staggered alignment, suggesting the presence of two electron transfer pathways: Type-II and Z-scheme. Electron spin resonance (ESR) spectroscopy was further employed to verify the direction of electron transfer (Fig. 7d). The ESR signals of DMPO-•O2 − were found to be insignificant on ZnS, whereas no noticeable signal was observed on NM. This indicates that the HOMO potential of NM is more positive than the standard potential of O2/•O2 − (−0.33 eV). However, 2ZCNM exhibited a significant •O2 − signal, suggesting the generation of active •O2 − species during photocatalysis. In the Type-II heterostructure, electrons transfer from the CB of ZnS to the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of NM, accumulating in the LUMO of NM, which hinders the production of •O2 −. Conversely, in the Z-scheme heterostructure, more electrons are present in the CB of ZnS due to the effective interaction between the electrons in the LUMO of NM and the holes in the VB of ZnS. This facilitates the separation of carriers in the space, leading to the generation of more •O2 − radicals [66,67]. Consequently, it can be concluded that 2ZCNM exhibits a Z-scheme heterostructure.

Based on the aforementioned analysis, the mechanism of 2ZCNM photocatalytic reduction of CO2 is illustrated in Fig. 8 . Under visible light irradiation, NM and ZnS generate electron-hole pairs. Due to the favorable electron transfer properties of CQDs and the proximity between the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of NM and the valence band (VB) of ZnS, a portion of the electrons from the LUMO of NM transfers to the VB of ZnS, complex with the holes and allowing more electrons to be retained in the conduction band (CB) of ZnS. These energetic electrons then migrate to the catalyst's surface, where they react with CO2, resulting in the formation of CH3OH and CH3CH2OH. Simultaneously, the presence of abundant holes in the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) of NM induces the generation of hydroxyl radicals (·OH), facilitating the spatial separation of charges. This process exhibits high reversibility and reproducibility, thereby accelerating and maximizing charge transfer and separation. Consequently, the lifetime of photogenerated electrons is significantly prolonged, leading to an enhanced photocatalytic reduction performance of CO2. The Z-scheme heterostructure process undergoes the following photochemical reaction steps when exposed to visible light irradiation:
Z n S
(
e
−
+
h
+
)
/ C Q D s / N M
(
e
−
+
h
+
)
+ h v → Z n S
(
e
−
)
/ C Q D s / N M
(
h
+
)
C
O 2
+ 6
H
+
+ 6
e
−
→ C
H 3
O H +
H 2
O
2 C
O 2
+ 12
H
+
+ 12
e
−
→ C
H 3
C
H 2
O H + 3
H 2
O
H 2
O + 4
h
+
→ · O H +
H
+

In this study, we successfully developed a new Z-scheme ZnS/CQDs/NH2−MIL-125(Ti) heterostructure photocatalyst. This heterostructure effectively suppressed the recombination of photogenerated carriers, broadened the absorption range, and enhanced the photocatalytic activity for CO2 reduction. The combination of ZnS with NH2−MIL-125(Ti) created a favorable energy band structure with high redox ability. CQDs expedited the separation and transfer of photogenerated electrons and holes. ZnS/CQDs/NH2−MIL-125(Ti) demonstrated remarkable photocatalytic activity, achieving yields of 501.57 µmol·g−1 and 564.70 µmol·g−1 for CH3OH and CH3CH2OH, respectively, in the reduction of CO2. Additionally, we provided a detailed analysis of the charge transfer pathway and photocatalytic CO2 reduction mechanism. Reaction pathways and reactant changes will be focused on in future research. This cost-effective photocatalysis holds significant promise as a simple technology for waste sludge resource recovery and the synthesis of hydrocarbon fuels from CO2.

Jun Zhou: Conceptualization, Data curation, Writing – review & editing. Qinwei Jia: Conceptualization, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Lei Gong: Conceptualization, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Shijun Zhang: Data curation, Validation. Xiangyu Zhou: Visualization. Hanwen Song: Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
